name: Tishtrya
description: Tishtrya, in Zoroastrianism, is the Avestan name of a divinity associated with the star Sirius and, more generally, rainfall and fertility. He is a Yazata, a benevolent divine being worthy of worship, and plays a crucial role in the cosmological battle against drought and chaos. Tishtrya's importance lies in his ability to ensure the earth's fertility through abundant rain, which is vital for agriculture and sustenance. He is often depicted as a magnificent white stallion with golden ears, battling the demon Apaosha, who embodies drought. The struggle between Tishtrya and Apaosha is a recurring motif, representing the eternal conflict between good and evil, order and chaos. The victory of Tishtrya heralds the arrival of life-giving rains, ensuring prosperity and well-being for humankind. Sacrifices and prayers are offered to Tishtrya to secure his favor and guarantee the continuation of the cycle of life. His veneration is deeply intertwined with the agricultural practices and the dependence on rainfall in ancient Persia.
subtitle: The Radiant Star

keyMyths.title: The Battle with Apaosha
keyMyths.description: The most prominent myth involving Tishtrya is his epic battle against the demon Apaosha, the embodiment of drought. For three days and nights, they clash in the cosmic ocean, Tishtrya in the form of a magnificent white stallion and Apaosha as a fearsome black horse. The outcome of this struggle determines whether the land will receive rain or suffer from drought. Ultimately, with the aid of prayers and sacrifices from humankind, Tishtrya triumphs, breaking the power of Apaosha and releasing the life-giving waters to nourish the earth. This victory is celebrated as a triumph of good over evil.

keyMyths.title: Tishtrya's Transformation
keyMyths.description: Before engaging Apaosha, Tishtrya initially appears as a weak and insignificant young man. He is disheartened by the lack of worship and sacrifices offered to him. However, upon realizing the importance of his role and being reminded of his divine power, he transforms into the powerful white stallion, ready to confront Apaosha. This transformation symbolizes the importance of faith and devotion in empowering the divine forces to overcome evil and maintain cosmic balance. It highlights the reciprocal relationship between humans and the divine in Zoroastrian cosmology.

keyMyths.title: The Distribution of Rain
keyMyths.description: Following his victory over Apaosha, Tishtrya is responsible for distributing the life-giving rain across the land. He ensures that all regions receive adequate moisture, promoting fertility and abundance. This act of benevolence underscores his role as a provider and sustainer of life. The rain brought by Tishtrya is not merely physical water; it is seen as a divine blessing that revitalizes the earth and ensures the prosperity of the Zoroastrian community. This myth reinforces the importance of respecting and appreciating the natural world.
